text: We present our experience with laparoscopic radical nephrectomy with inferior vena cava thrombectomy. We present the case of a sixty five year old male patient with no relevant clinical history. He complained of progressive loss of weight up to ten kilograms, edema in both lower limbs, and denied hematuria or pain. CT scan of abdomen and pelvis showed a solid infiltrating mass occupying the middle and lower third of the right kidney, which enhanced with contrast. Invasion of the right renal vein and inferior vena cava below the diaphragm was present.

The patient is positioned in lateral decubitus. Three trocars are inserted along the midline: two 12mm and one 5mm, with an additional 12mm subcostal trocar. The surgeon releases the lower border of the liver and identifies the inferior vena cava.

The surgeon dissects the right renal artery at the intercaval aortic level and applies early clamping. Four hepatic veins, tributaries of the caudate lobe, are identified on the anterior face of the inferior vena cava, and are ligated and sectioned.

The surgeon progresses above the upper limit of the thrombus in the inferior vena cava and places a Rommel's tourniquet around the vessel. The tourniquet is then adjusted, and the vascular clamps are removed.
In conclusion, laparoscopic radical nephrectomy with inferior vena cava thrombectomy is a challenging but feasible procedure in selected patients.
